data.title: U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) On Pace To Break 97.00 - Why Is The Dollar Falling Ahead Of The FOMC?
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The dollar (DXY) has taken quite a significant hit after its strong start to 2026.

This is precisely what happens when technicals align with changing fundamentals. As noted in our pre-Greenland chaos analysis, the Dollar Index was already showing signs of imminent technical weakness.

So, when Donald Trump decided not only to launch an investigation into Jerome Powell but also to threaten his historic allies, what was seen as a slow, progressive dedollarization quickly became a catastrophe for the US dollar.

Some European funds are selling their dollar-denominated debt assets in concern over new, aggressive policies from the current administration, and by actively seeking alternatives, reducing dollar demand. This is leading, in part, to the current decline.

Combined with a seasonal tendency for the US dollar to drop ahead of interest rate decisions during cutting cycles, the weekly drop is getting extreme. Fewer participants can absorb sudden outflows ahead of FOMC meetings for risk management reasons, amplifying such moves.

This de-dollarization explains the ongoing run in gold (which just hit $5,000 today) and other metals - the debasement trade, for those unfamiliar with the trending financial term.

Looking back at the September cut, for example, the Dollar Index had reached 2025 yearly lows, a fast-paced selloff just two days ahead of the rate decision.

The current situation shows similar conditions, despite no rate cuts anticipated. What interests traders is whether the selloff will continue after the FOMC.

For additional foundational context, I strongly encourage you to explore our FOMC preview.

With the Fed Funds rate expected to be kept unchanged, investors and institutions will be listening closely to Powell's speech.

A bit less than two rate cuts are currently priced for 2026. With labor conditions seemingly worsening only slightly and inflation remaining closer to 3% than 2% (despite some improvements), the Fed Chair doesn't have many reasons to turn dovish, but the current pricing is still reasonable.

Essentially, the more resilient US economy supports the dollar and could lead to sudden inflows back into the greenback after the meeting.

The difference maker will be found in unpredictable events:

  • The nomination of the next Fed Chair could have a significant influence on the dollar demand (particularly if Rick Rieder gets selected).
  • If Trump moves to intervene in Iran, the dollar should appreciate suddenly in pro-dollar risk-averse market conditions - kind of similar to what happened after Venezuela.
  • If Trump actually pushes his intense rhetoric further with allies, however, the dollar outflows will be severe - you would see the results in the Dollar Index flashing below 2025 lows.
  • The FOMC event itself could support the USD but would depend on Powell's tone regarding his 2026 outlook.

While we're here, let's see what the charts say in our multi-time frame analysis of the US Dollar Index (DXY) to see if there is still much left in the ongoing down move.

Dollar Index (DXY) Multi-Time Frame Analysis

Daily Chart

The technical picture changed suddenly over the past week.

Bulls were taking the index back towards the 99.50 level, but with some short-time frame resistances, bear divergences combined with Trump actually pushing the Greenland theme, the fused technicals and fundamentals had an immediate effect on the DXY, down 2.50% until today.

Last week led to a huge gap lower today, with the pre-FOMC position closing effect pushing the index to test the 96.50-97.00 support.

Whether it holds or breaks in the next 1.5 sessions doesn't matter much; the most important will be to see if the dollar remains above or below after the FOMC.

  • Closing above 97.00 should lead to a slow but consistent rebound back towards 99.00.
  • Below, however, opens the door to test the 2025 lows.
    • These scenarios are not considering any black swan events.

4H Chart and Technical Levels

Looking closer, the question remains whether the gap is an exhaustion/low-volume gap (implying that an extreme is reached) or whether this is an actual runaway gap (meaning further downside).

To help tilt the scales, it is essential to track the path of least resistance.

With the 4H RSI in extreme oversold territory and a key support coming into effect, a rebound makes sense. The question is when.

Keep in mind that the buying could still not be so sudden, as traders remain on the sidelines ahead of the key risk events coming. Think of how such views could be expressed in different FX pairs.

Levels to place on your DXY charts:

Resistance Levels

  • August range bull/bear pivot 97.25-97.60
  • 98.00 main support turned minor resistance
  • Higher time frame pivotal resistance 98.80-99.00
  • 99.40-99.50 January resistance (last Friday levels)
  • 100.376 November highs

Support Levels

  • 2025 lows major support 96.50-97.00
  • Session lows 96.80
  • September FOMC lows 96.20
  • Early 2022 consolidation just below 96.00
  • 95.00 main psychological support

1H Chart

Looking closer, one thing looks clear - the downside is stalling after a brutal descent.

But a slowdown in a downtrend doesn't imply an imminent rebound. Buyers will first have to show up.

With the selloff stalling at the descending channel lows, imminent downside keeps a lower probability setup.

Hence, from here, a consolidation range until the FOMC between 96.80 and 97.30 is highly probable.

After the FOMC, however, the rest will be to see if bulls show up for an upside breakout (to at least test the upper bound of the channel ~98.20).

In case they don't, the selloff may continue.

Safe Trades!

data.title: Lazard Developing Markets Equity Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Equity markets in the developing world, as measured by the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, rose 4.7% in the fourth quarter, as risk appetites remained largely resilient amid investor enthusiasm for artificial intelligence (AI) and despite tariff risks and geopolitical risks.

Latin America was the best-performing region, climbing 8.2%. Stock markets in Chile and Peru gained in sympathy with the rise in commodity prices. Brazil's stock market rose, thanks to interest rate cuts locally and in the US. Argentina's stock market jumped on news that libertarian President Javier Milei won a landslide victory in midterm elections, handing him a mandate to continue pursuing his radical overhaul of the country's economy.

The region encompassing emerging Europe, the Middle East, and Africa (EMEA) was the worst-performing region, rising 4.0%. Stock markets in Poland, Hungary, and the Czech Republic rose, as investors began to anticipate better economic prospects and a possibility of a peace agreement between Russia and Ukraine. South Africa's stock market rose in sympathy with the rise in the price of gold.

Emerging Asia also underperformed, advancing 4.5%. Stock markets in global technology hubs Korea and Taiwan rose on optimism about the demand outlook for chips, the flagship industry of both countries, despite wavering sentiment in November. China's stock market fell on concerns about the country's economic outlook.

Information technology, materials, and energy were the best-performing sectors in the period. Consumer discretionary, health care and communication services were the worst-performing sectors.

(Index returns were measured net of taxes and in US dollar terms. )

Portfolio Review

In the fourth quarter, the Lazard Developing Markets Equity Portfolio rose in absolute terms but underperformed its benchmark, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index. (Excess return was measured net of fees and in US dollar terms).

Helped

  • Stock selection in industrials and utilities sectors boosted relative performance, as did stock selection in Korea and Taiwan.
  • Higher-than-benchmark exposures to the information technology sector and to Taiwan contributed.
  • Lower-than-benchmark exposures to the communication services sector and to Saudi Arabia and China were beneficial to relative performance.
  • Shares of Korea-based chipmaker Samsung Electronics (7.1% Portfolio weighting) and Hynix (3.16% Portfolio weighting) rose in sympathy with the rising prices of memory chips
  • Shares of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (13.4% Portfolio weighting) traded higher on the back of strong demand for the Taiwan-based contract chipmaker's advanced process node manufacturing from its high-performance computing (HPC) customers.
  • Shares of ASE Technology (2.3% Portfolio weighting) rose after the Taiwan-based chip packaging and testing provider reported better-than-expected third-quarter results, anchored by robust AI-fueled demand for its services. Shares received an additional boost after the company issued an upbeat outlook due to the AI-related demand.
  • Shares in Bizlink (1.4% Portfolio weighting) climbed on bullish sentiment about the Taiwan-based wire harness maker's earnings outlook, anchored by strong demand in power interconnect products.

Hurt

  • Stock selection in the consumer discretionary and information technology sectors weighed on relative performance, as did stock selection in China and Brazil.
  • Higher-than-benchmark exposure to the consumer discretionary sector detracted.
  • Lower-than-benchmark exposures to the materials and financials sectors and to Korea and Taiwan were detrimental to relative performance.
  • Shares of e-commerce giant Alibaba Group (BABA) fell on profit-taking (4.0% Portfolio weighting) in the wake of AI-fueled enthusiasm in China.
  • Shares of China-based social media and games company Tencent (5.7% Portfolio weighting) retreated on profit taking after a period of strong stock price appreciation.
  • The fall in the stock price of Singapore-based internet platform operator Sea (0.7% Portfolio weighting) was attributed to concerns about the current period of higher investments.
  • Shares of Xiaomi (1.4% Portfolio weighting) fell on concerns about the China's smartphone and electric vehicle (EV) manufacturer's earnings outlook due to rising memory chip prices, a recent fatal crash involving one of its cars, and a longer-than-expected wait period for approval of its second EV factory.
  • Shares of Lenovo (1.5% Portfolio weighting) fell on concerns about the China-based personal computer and data center equipment maker's earning outlook due to the rising costs of memory would hurt margins and demand in 2026.

Outlook

Most market fundamentals remain fluid based upon significant geopolitical and trade risks as well as valuations. Our expectations will vary based upon these issues, but, at this juncture, we remain reasonably constructive on emerging markets as long as global growth remains balanced.

data.title: 1 Main Capital Partners Q4 2025 Fund Letter
data.description:
Dear Partners,

For the fourth quarter, 1 Main Capital Partners, L. P. ("1MC" or the "Fund") returned 3.4%, compared to 2.7% and 2.2% for the S&P 500 and Russell 2000, respectively. 1

2025 Inception
Cumulative Annualized
1 Main Capital Partners - net 20.1% 346.9% 20.8%
S&P 500 17.9% 176.0% 13.7%
Russell 2000 12.8% 74.6% 7.3%
ITD is from the Fund's inception, February 1, 2018.

1MC's goal remains to generate strong long-term performance, without taking on excess risk. In the context of such an objective, there is little use reading too much into a given year. Fortunately, our since-inception results reflect sustained progress towards achieving that goal and validation of the strategy.

1MC's three largest holdings in 2025 were our largest winners (IWG, BFit and DNTL), contributing a combined 30% gross (25% net). DNTL was acquired by GTCR while we continue to hold material positions in IWG and BFit, which account for just under half of our capital.

Meanwhile, the Fund's three largest detractors in 2025 (SUP, WATR, PRKR) cost us a combined 6% gross (5% net). We sold SUP in May after it lost its two largest customers, before it was taken over by lenders. Our quick exit saved us from losing an additional 1% of our capital on the position. We also sold WATR, after I lost faith in management, and added to PRKR, about which I remain optimistic. Lastly, we added substantially to our position in LMB, making it a core holding for 1MC once again.

As has been the case over the last eight years, I continue to size my highest conviction core investments aggressively (5-25% of capital) and opportunistic bets as options (typically 1-3% of capital). In each case, I consider not just the expected upside, but also potential downside – an approach that has served us well.

Looking forward to 2026, I am excited to welcome a new analyst. With an MBA from Columbia Business School and nearly a decade of public equity buyside experience, I expect he will ramp quickly, allowing me to significantly accelerate the velocity of ideas evaluated for our portfolio. He starts next month.

Current Positioning

At quarter-end, the Fund's top five positions were Basic-Fit (BFit NA), International Workplace Group (IWG), Limbach Holdings (LMB), Radiant Logistics (RLGT) and our ski/wake basket (MCFT and MBUU), which together accounted for approximately 65% of capital.

Round Three: Limbach Holdings (LMB)

Limbach Holdings (LMB) was the Fund's top contributor in 2020 and 2023 and has been our most lucrative investment since inception. In Q4, I added significantly to our position, once again making it a core holding for the Fund, after its shares declined 55% from their 2025 high due to concerns around a temporary demand slowdown in Q2 and Q3, largely driven by uncertainty surrounding the 2025 government shutdown.

As a reminder, LMB is a leading building systems solution firm that partners with owners of mission-critical infrastructure to offer design, engineering, maintenance, and repair services. The company specializes in mechanical, electrical, and plumbing systems for customers in six core verticals: healthcare, industrial/manufacturing, data centers, life sciences, higher education, and entertainment.

Since coming public in 2016, LMB has transitioned from bidding almost entirely through general contractors (GCR) to owner-direct relationships (ODR). For 2025, the company's guidance suggests that ODR will have accounted for approximately 75% of its revenues and a greater share of gross profits.

The merits of this shift are clear: ODR projects are smaller, higher-margin, less capital-intensive, and less cyclical than GCR work. Roughly one-third of LMB's ODR revenue comes from high-velocity maintenance or fixed-price repairs under $10k, while the rest is project-based work with an average size of $250k—a stark contrast to the average GCR project of $2-3 million. Crucially, ODR clients view LMB as a trusted strategic partner essential to their daily operations, whereas general contractors often view the firm as a replaceable subcontractor.

This transition has driven LMB's EBITDA margins from 3% in 2019 to 12% today. While consolidated revenue growth was muted during this period as the company walked away from lower-quality GCR work, I believe we are now at an inflection point where organic revenue growth will accelerate while margin expansion remains steady.

Additionally, LMB has very little debt and is highly cash generative with a robust acquisition pipeline. It has proven that it can acquire GCR-heavy businesses at just 5x EBITDA and transition them to ODR. This "buy and transform" strategy further reduces its entry multiple and represents an exceptional use of FCF.

Between organic ODR growth and accretive acquisitions, I believe LMB can grow FCF per share at approximately a 20% CAGR over the medium term without the need for financial leverage. As the company grows, diversifies its geographic footprint, and becomes better understood by the broader market, I expect growth in both its earnings power and multiple.

Outlook

As we head into 2026, economic indicators appear strong and broader indices are at levels that suggest a degree of optimism regarding interest rates and the durability of growth. Specifically, mega-caps and new-economy winners are priced at valuations that leave little room for misstep. Conversely, there remains a pool of smaller, old-economy businesses that remain ignored and mispriced. The fact that smaller companies as a group have consistently underperformed larger companies since the Fund's inception has provided us with opportunities to acquire high-quality businesses at multiples that I believe to be bargains.

While passive flows and "pod shops" dominate short-term price action, I remain confident that share prices follow the trajectory of free cash flow per share over the long term. Many of our top holdings currently generate double-digit FCF yields while reinvesting at high rates of return into long growth runways. These businesses are led by aligned management teams who take advantage of market dislocations to make acquisitions, widen their moats, and buy back shares.

For concentrated fundamental investors willing to do the work and able to build conviction, the market's shift toward passive and short-termism is a gift. It allows us to find attractive opportunities where high quality meets low valuation, and to concentrate our capital accordingly.

As we look toward 2026, 1MC remains focused on our best ideas. I continue to believe our portfolio is significantly more attractive than the indices. Additionally, I remain prepared to rebalance our capital into its highest and best use whenever the inevitable macro air pocket resurfaces.

I am grateful for your continued partnership and for the luxury of a long-term capital base that allows us to look past quarterly noise in pursuit of multi-year compounding. I remain incredibly excited about the future of 1MC.

Other Updates

The Fund remains open to capital from existing and new like-minded partners; notably, the Founder's Class has $10 million of capacity remaining. While the vast majority of my time is dedicated to research on current and prospective holdings, I continue to value referrals. Additionally, I will be attending several cap intro events in the coming months and would welcome the chance to meet in person.

  • • BTIG (Omaha, NE)
  • • Cannell Capital 3PM (Alta, WY)
  • • Jefferies (NYC)
  • • Raymond James (Virtual)
  • • Santangel's Roundtable (NYC)

Please reach out if you would like to connect at or around any of the above, or with any other thoughts or questions.

Sincerely,

Yaron Naymark

data.title: GE Aerospace: The Pros And Cons Of Investing In The Stock Right Now
data.description:
GE Aerospace (GE) designs and produces aircraft engines used both in the commercial and defense space. Their main products include integrated engine components, electric power, and mechanical aircraft systems. The company's key commercial - and probably most well-known - clients are Boeing (BA) and Airbus (EADSF).

The firm's stock performed exceptionally well over the past 12 months, significantly outperforming the broader market (SPY), despite the sharp decline in the share price in the past week, just after the earnings release.

The aim of this article today is to take a look at GE's business, including the latest financial results and the firm's valuation, to assess whether it is worth buying the current dip or not.

Pros

Growth

When talking about GE Aerospace and the main investment theme around the stock, the first thing that comes to people's minds now is the company's outstanding growth figures. In the most recent quarter, the firm managed to beat analyst estimates on both the top and bottom lines. Revenue came in at $11.9 billion, or $70 million above analyst expectations. This figure represents a sales growth of 20.4% year-over-year.

If we break down the revenue into segments and sources of revenue, we can see that both of the reportable segments, Commercial Engines & Services - CES - and Defense & Propulsion Technologies - DPT, grew.

CES grew by a whopping 24% compared to the prior year. It was the result of a 31% growth from services revenue, totaling $7 billion for the quarter, and a 7% growth from equipment sales. The drivers of the service revenue growth were internal shop visits, where volume and work scopes both increased. The growth from equipment sales was fueled by volume - mainly driven by the LEAP engine sales - and partially offset by lower spare engine ratios. For those of you who are not familiar with the term LEAP engine, here is a brief description of the technology:

The LEAP engine family from CFM International-a consortium comprising GE Aerospace and Safran Aircraft Engines-includes three different models covering a thrust range from 23,000 to 35,000 pounds. Among the aircraft they power are the Airbus A320neo and the Boeing 737 MAX."

DPT - a much smaller segment than CES - grew at a slow but still double-digit rate year-over-year. Growth came in at 13%, mainly fuelled by equipment sales growth - 18% - while services revenue grew by 7% year-over-year. If we break down the segment even further, we can see that Defense & Systems only grew by 2%, while Propulsion and Additive Technologies increased by as much as 33%.

It is not only the firm's top line that showed exceptional performance. As a result of the revenue growth, lower interest expense, lower share count, and adjusted ETR, adjusted EPS increased by 38% y/y for the full year and 19% y/y for Q4.

Backlog

Results of the past quarter and past year are all stories of the past, but if we look forward, we can still have an idea of how the firm's revenue and earnings might shape up in the near term. One line item to look at is the backlog.

Orders for both reportable segments skyrocketed in the past quarter. In total, there was a 74% increase, which was a result of a 76% growth in CES and 61% growth in DPT.

Looking at revenue, operating profit, EPS, and backlog growth, we can see why there was significant enthusiasm from growth-focused investors in the past months and why GE's share price outperformed the rest of the market so significantly. But if the performance was so good, why did the share price drop after the earnings release? Well, there are a few things that likely concerned investors, and these include slowing growth, margin compression, valuation, and tariff uncertainties. So let us look at these right now.

Cons

Slowing growth

Looking forward to 2026, revenue and operating profit growth are anticipated to slow.

While CES services revenue growth was 26% in 2025, it is expected to be "only" in the mid-teens range for 2026. CES equipment revenue growth is expected to remain in line with the 2025 figures, but it is a significantly smaller segment. The overall revenue growth of CES is expected to slow from 24% to the mid-teens.

DPT revenue growth is also expected to slow. In 2025 growth was 11%, and for the 2026, mid- to high-single-digit growth figures are expected.

Despite the huge increase in the backlog, revenue growth is expected to slow, which in most cases leads to a multiple compression and therefore prompts a share price decline - which we could see after the earnings announcement.

Margin compression

It is not only the slowing growth that could appear troubling, but also the decline in profitability. Both reportable segments experienced lower operating margins. The decline in CES was much more meaningful, 420 bps year-over-year, driven by lower spare engine ratio, OE growth, and investments.

The margin compression in DPT was 70 bps compared to the prior year, driven by investments and inflation.

Valuation

The following table summarizes a set of traditional price multiples and compares GE's figures with those of the sector median and the company's historic valuation metrics. GE stock is selling at a significantly higher multiple than the rest of the industrial sector. According to a few metrics, however, it is selling at a discount compared to the historic figures. As growth slows, I believe the discount compared to the historic multiples is justified.

If we narrow down the peer group to firms in the aerospace and defense industry, we can see that GE's stock is roughly in line with their valuations. There are two metrics, however, and according to those, GE appears much more expensive. These are P/CF and P/S.

So what is my takeaway on the valuation?

Despite growth slowing, growth is still strong, so the current multiples may even be justified. But I do not believe that there is a significant upside from the current price point, and I do not believe that the stock is an attractive value play. In case margins compress further or growth slows, I expect price multiples to follow as well.

Tariff uncertainty

Tariff uncertainty is something that I write a lot about nowadays. Because you basically never know what will happen the next day and which sector, industry, or firm will be impacted. For example, Canada was recently threatened with 100% tariffs on Canada. There were threats also voiced last week, driven by the disagreement on Greenland between the U.S. and the U.K. These threats, however, do not seem to be a concern anymore, as the U.S. walked back on these.

All in all, the threats are creating an environment of constant uncertainty for businesses, making it extremely difficult to foresee and anticipate what to expect in the coming quarters and years. It is not only a risk for GE, but for most firms, and therefore a risk for the market as a whole.

Conclusion

GE Aerospace delivered exceptional growth in Q4 and in 2025 overall. Revenue, operating profit, EPS, and orders backlog all grew meaningfully. Despite these figures, however, the share price plummeted after the earnings release.

Main concerns going forward include slowing growth in both of the reportable segments, the development of profitability metrics, and the impact of potential tariffs.

While the current valuation does not seem extremely high, I do not see significant upside potential from the current price levels, and I do believe a potential multiple compression could be on the table if growth slows faster than expected or profitability deteriorates further.

All in all, I rate GE's stock as a hold.

data.title: First Foundation: Limited Progress And Deterioration Justify A Downgrade
data.description:
It seems like just yesterday that I last wrote about First Foundation (FFWM). But looking back at my rating history of the stock, my last article about it was published way back in May of 2024. In that article, I discussed how I made the decision to downgrade the stock from a ‘buy’ to a ‘hold’. This was based on my view that caution was warranted. The company was experiencing a decline in profitability, an increase in debt, and declining deposits. Net interest income and net profits dropped materially. And although the stock was cheap, it was not cheap enough to justify the risks involved.

Since then, the company has continued to shrink. And honestly, the stock has performed better than I would have expected in light of these circumstances. Perhaps driven by the fact that there have been some small improvements as of late, the stock is actually up 7.3% since that article was published. By comparison, the S&P 500 is up a solid 32.1%. But even though we have seen some improvements, I think that severe caution is appropriate right now. In fact, I would go so far as to say that downgrading the stock to a ‘sell’ is the right choice in this environment.

Naturally, my opinion could change as new data comes in. We are fortunate enough that, right around the corner, management will be announcing financial results covering the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. That will occur before the market opens on January 29th. But even leading up to that point, I don't see any reasons to be optimistic. That's why I am sticking to my decision to downgrade the stock.

This picture has been painful

Over the last few years now, First Foundation has undergone some major changes. Management has been actively working to restructure the business. But that doesn't change the fact that this has involved significant shrinkage. From 2022 through 2024, deposits on the company's books dropped sharply from $10.36 billion to $9.87 billion. And by the third quarter of 2025, deposits had fallen even more to $9.29 billion. This recent drop for 2025 is in spite of the fact that there was a slight uptick in brokered deposits for the company from $3.2 billion to $3.3 billion. One really big change, however, involved uninsured deposits. At the end of 2024, First Foundation had $2.40 billion worth. That translated to approximately 24.3% of overall deposits. By the third quarter of last year, this had dropped to $1.90 billion, or about 20.5%. Both of these are better than the 30% maximum that I typically like to see. But in general, less exposure is better than more exposure.

As deposits decreased, the value of loans dropped also. This makes perfect sense. Deposits are the literal fuel that banks use in order to grow. And when those deposits drop, unless something else changes such as equity issuances, an increase in debt, or changes in asset balances elsewhere, loans must ultimately drop. Unfortunately, the company was essentially forced into this situation. As the Federal Reserve began increasing interest rates, aimed at combating inflationary pressures, the institution found itself stuck with significant amounts of fixed rate loans. We don't know the exact composition because management never revealed it. But we do know that about $2.58 billion of its land and construction and commercial and industrial loans were fixed. A lot of their mortgage loans were variable, but with initial fixed windows of time. Some of those extended up to 10 years.

As the Federal Reserve began hiking interest rates, these loans started to become a problem because, in order to keep depositors on its books, it had to hike how much it was paying for deposits. For instance, in 2022, interest bearing deposits cost the company only 1.04%. But by 2023, that had spiked to 3.79%. Meanwhile, general borrowings grew from 2.87% to 4.67%, while subordinated debt increased from 3.88% to 3.94%. On the whole, interest bearing liabilities went from costing the company 1.27% to 3.90%. By comparison, the return on interest earning assets grew much more modestly from 3.69% to 4.50%.

Management is not as detailed, or at least not consistently detailed, as I would like. But we do know that, by the middle of last year, 85% of its overall loans were structured as variable. Unfortunately, now that interest rates are dropping, that's not ideal. Of course, effective returns on variable rate assets will adjust lower at a slower pace than deposits will. So that will see some push up in net interest margin. But that would be short lived, especially if interest rates continue to fall. It is worth noting that, as the image above illustrates, the company is at least diverse. But the biggest area of concentration would be multifamily properties that accounted for $3.27 billion of its overall loans.

The value of securities has actually risen. The firm went from having $1.09 billion worth in 2022 to having $2.20 billion worth in the third quarter of 2025. Again, the shift here has been toward improving net interest margin. Cash and cash equivalents have also increased, jumping from $656.5 million to $1.73 billion. But the downside of this is that debt has also grown. The firm went from having $1.37 billion earth back in 2022 to having $1.60 billion worth today.

The changes in the balance sheet for the company have had a huge impact on revenue and profits. Net interest income fell sharply from $318.2 million in 2022 to $161.9 million in 2024. And for the first nine months of 2025, the drop to $77.1 million was from $131.3 million the previous year. The drop in the overall size of the balance sheet was problematic. But also, for most of this time, net interest margin fell sharply as well. This can be seen in the chart below. Fortunately, the first nine months of 2025 did see an increase, mostly because of the repositioning of the company's balance sheet and declining interest rates.

Because the company has experienced some major changes, non-interest income has also been incredibly volatile. In fact, in 2024, it was negative to the tune of $65.9 million. And for the first nine months of that year, it was negative to the tune of $79.2 million. That stemmed from the fact that, during that window of time, the company incurred a loss of $115.8 million associated with capital market activities. This was mostly because of lower of cost or market adjustments associated with the transfer of a $1.9 billion portfolio of multifamily loans that were held for investment to loans held for sale. We have seen improvement in 2025 because of the absence of this adjustment. But that's not enough to prevent the net profits of the company from coming in negative by $147.1 million during the first nine months of 2025.

Because of the profitability issues that the company has had, with 2025 likely being the third consecutive year of net losses, we can't really value the company in relation to earnings. But in the chart above, you can see how it's priced on a price to book and price to tangible book basis. The firm is incredibly cheap in this regard, coming in below any of the five banks that I compared it to in that chart. And normally, this would be attractive to me. But still, we are looking at a firm that is under significant pressure right now because of all the challenges it has faced over the last few years.

Asset quality is also abysmal. In the first chart above, you can see its return on assets compared to the return on assets of the same five institutions I stacked up against. And in the second chart abve, you can see its return on equity. It is, by far, the worst of the group. Credit quality is also less than ideal. According to management, 0.94% of the company’s loans were classified as non-performing during the most recent quarter. That's almost double the 0.50% maximum that I typically like to see.

Even though I am taking a bearish stance on the business, it is possible that my assessment could change as new data comes in. And it just so happens that new data will be coming out before the market opens on January 29th. Leading up to that point, analysts don't seem all that optimistic either though. They expect revenue to fall from $64.7 million last year to $58.4 million this year. They do, however, expect earnings per share to improve from negative $0.17 to positive $0.03, which would take net profits up from negative $14.1 million to positive $2.5 million. But given the volatility that the company has exhibited over time, I don't think a single solid quarter is enough to stoke optimism.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I will say that I appreciate what management has been working on. But right now, I don't find this to be the most compelling prospect. The company has had problems in recent years, and even though we have seen some progress, financial performance is still challenging. Add on top of this the fact that the declining interest rate environment could actually hurt the company long term because of its heavy shift into variable assets, and I think that downgrading it to a ‘sell’ is the right choice.

data.title: Davis Financial Fund Annual Review 2026
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Davis Financial Fund (DFF) returned +29.29% in 2025, exceeding the performance of the S&P Financials Index which returned +15.02% and the S&P 500 Index, which returned +17.88%.
  • $10, 000 invested in Davis Financial Fund grew to $503, 581 vs. $265, 188 for S&P 500 Financials Index since inception in 1991. The fund outperformed both S&P 500 Index and S&P 500 Financials Index since inception.
  • Banking, consumer lending and capital markets stocks performed well, supported by tailwinds across credit, spreads, expenses, and regulation. Investor sentiment towards financial stocks—in our view, long weighted down by memories
  • We believe our portfolio companies’ valuations remain sufficiently low to provide for attractive returns over the next decade, and are particularly attractive on a relative basis versus the broader market.
  • DFF has outperformed the S&P Financials Index by more than 300 basis points per year over the last five years and more than 200 basis points per year since the fund’s inception in 1991, serving as a powerful reminder of the value that active management can provide over passive index investing.

Strategy: Growth in Disguise

Since its inception in 1991, Davis Financial Fund (DFF) has invested in durable, well-managed financial services companies at value prices which the fund could hold for the long-term. Shelby Cullom Davis’s quip that financial services companies can be “growth companies in disguise” remains a bedrock tenet of our approach. Investors tend to place low valuations on financial companies because of their earnings volatility. However, many financial companies generate capital through the business cycle at an attractive rate which they use to pay dividends, buy back stock or otherwise deploy in ways that increase shareholder value. By focusing on economic reality rather than investor sentiment, DFF has compounded shareholder wealth at +11.96% annually over 34 years, outpacing both the S&P 500 Index and the S&P 500 Financials Index.

2025 Results

The S&P 500 Index returned +17.88% in 2025. It experienced a −19% peak-to-trough drawdown early in the year driven by fear and uncertainty principally around U. S. trade policy, but by the end of the year equity markets had stormed back and then some, making it the third year in a row of exceptional investment returns. While the U. S. has imposed its highest level of tariffs in decades, the impact on economic activity—at least so far—has been muted, and markets seem to have shrugged it off. Continued enthusiasm for the promise of artificial intelligence (AI), and the immense capital expenditures that go with it, drove further stock market gains for many technology companies.

The S&P Financials Index lagged the broader market with a return of +15.02%. The index return was driven by its large banks, capital markets firms and consumer lending companies. Its laggards included alternative asset managers, select payments companies and insurance brokers.

As noted, DFF outperformed both indices with a return of +29.29%. The biggest contributors to our relative performance were our payments and consumer lending companies (Capital One, American Express (AXP) and Rocket Companies (RKT)), foreign banks (Danske Bank, DBS Group and DNB Bank), selected U. S. banks (JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and Wells Fargo (WFC)) and capital markets companies (BNY Mellon (BK) and Julius Baer). The fund’s largest detractors from relative performance were its holding companies (Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) and Exor).

Market Environment: Sentiment Inflection Point?

We have observed frequently in these letters that we believed the valuations of financial stocks, and banks in particular, have been weighed down by the leeriness of investors with still-vivid memories of the 2008–09 financial crisis. With 2025 being the third year in a row of strong returns for financial stocks it may well mark the year that investors finally started warming up to the sector. The S&P Financials Index still lagged the performance of the broader market in 2025, and continues to trade at an historically deep discount as measured by price/earnings ratios. However, in absolute terms investment returns on financial stocks were strong in 2025, and their valuations have begun to reset higher.

The reset in valuations can be observed in figure 2 showing trends in the price-to-tangible book value multiple of several leading financial institutions. This multiple has expanded by over 70% on a simple average basis in the past three years, a period which included the regional banking crisis of 2023. If prices convey information, the implication of this market move should reflect some combination of the following: book values are perceived to be more “solid” (i. e. , there aren’t as yet unrecognized credit losses suspected around the corner), the outlook for business growth has improved, there has been a step-up in the expected long-term profitability of the businesses (i. e. , higher returns on equity, including from any reductions in capital requirements), and/or the market has reduced the rate of return investors require of these stocks in discounting their future cash flows (which is to say, investor sentiment towards the group is improving).

We think probably all these factors have helped drive market returns in financial stocks, particularly banks, as there have been tailwinds across multiple dimensions. With respect to credit, the trends have been largely stable. Consumer-related losses have “normalized” upward from their low levels in the aftermath of COVID but are still trending better today than a year ago. Commercial non-performing loans on bank balance sheets have remained low and steady (despite a few high-profile exceptions last fall). In the commercial office space, banks are recognizing charge-offs but these are in line with the loss reserves previously established (i. e. , no further impact on earnings or book value).

On profitability, interest spreads have begun widening again as the banks’ fixed rate assets have been rolling over at higher yields, revealing the attractive economics of their low-cost deposit franchises. Furthermore, banks have generally been able to slow the rate of growth in their expenses of late, in part through investment in their technology infrastructure (and the adoption of AI into their labor-intensive operations could cement this trend for some time to come). Many banks are currently generating returns on tangible equity in the mid-to-high teens, and management comments and targets suggest that this level should be sustainable in the medium term (or longer), as shown in figure 3.

The regulatory environment has been moving in a favorable direction as well (although arguably this was being discounted in stock prices already at the end of last year). Capital rules being finalized seem set to be far less onerous than they would have been under the prior administration, which contributes to managements’ optimistic outlooks for their ROEs. Regulators are more willing to consider M&A transactions, and there has been relief on certain supervisory limitations.

We try in our work to differentiate between intrinsic value and market value. Some of the trends observed in 2025 do represent positive changes to our estimate of intrinsic value at our portfolio companies. But many of them have been incorporated into our analysis of intrinsic value for quite some time, and it is only now that the market is seemingly recognizing them. Hence, the strong market returns experienced over the last year make it fair to say that the gap between intrinsic value and market value at many financial companies—although it still remains—has narrowed.

Portfolio Positioning: Trimming and Redeploying

Banks, both domestic and foreign, continue to represent the majority of our holdings in the DFF portfolio. However, given their strong market performance during the year, we have trimmed our exposure to banks (primarily BNY Mellon, DBS and Danske) and redeployed that capital into property & casualty reinsurers (Everest Group (EG) and RenaissanceRe (RNR)), select payments-related companies (Fiserv (FI) and Chime) and a family-controlled investment holding company, Exor.

Many of our core holdings remain the same as a year ago. Capital One continues to be the largest position in the fund. Its transformational acquisition of Discover Financial (DFS) closed in May 2025. In addition to targeting annual cost synergies of $1.5 billion, management is anticipating so-called “network” synergies of $1.2 billion from transitioning certain Capital One debit and credit card volumes into Discover’s networks. Importantly, the latter synergy target is based on transitioning only a minority of Capital One’s credit card volume. Longer-term, we think the company has an opportunity to continue integrating its card-issuing activities with its card network. Looking out a few years we believe Capital One remains attractively priced at less than 10x earnings despite the potential, in our view, to earn +/−20% returns on tangible capital on average and over time.

Wells Fargo is the largest “traditional” bank in our portfolio. The company passed a milestone this year with the removal of the asset cap imposed by the Federal Reserve that has been a governor on its growth. Just as importantly, Wells Fargo has made progress over several years in right-sizing its cost structure. It has also seen positive momentum in the business lines it has recently been investing into (investment banking, credit cards and wealth management). The bank has a surplus of capital which it is working down through share repurchases, which will enhance earnings per share growth over the medium term. Despite the stock’s +36% return in 2025, Wells Fargo’s valuation at 2.1x tangible book value remains reasonable for a bank that ought to earn a high-teens return on tangible equity over time. The other so-called “super-regional” banks in the portfolio (Fifth Third Bank, U. S. Bancorp and PNC Financial) are trading at similar or better valuations, and we remain optimistic about their prospective returns.

Chubb (CB) is one of our core property & casualty insurance holdings. It is well-diversified across products and geographies. The company has consistently generated returns on equity comfortably ahead of the industry owing to a combination of running advantaged lines of business with a disciplined underwriting and operating culture. Pricing trends in the insurance markets have generally been strong in recent years, and consequently Chubb has been earning returns on tangible equity in the low 20s. While competitive forces may in time push that back toward a “normalized” level a few points lower, Chubb we believe would still be valued at 10–11x earnings looking out a few years.

After three consecutive years of strong investment returns in financials, it’s inevitable that the gap between market price and our estimate of intrinsic value would narrow. However, since the portfolio in aggregate is valued at approximately 13x this year’s earnings—a significant discount to both the broader S&P 500 Index and the S&P Financials Index—we continue to believe it is positioned to generate attractive returns over the next decade.

Outlook: Durable Across Outcomes

We remain consistent in our approach to allocating capital in our portfolio—that is, we look for companies with durable competitive advantages coupled with competent and honest managements that are priced at a discount to their intrinsic value. We invest presuming that we will own our companies through business cycles. We do not attempt to build a portfolio around a particular speculative forecast—for example, by trying to predict where interest rates or the economy will go. Rather, we strive to construct a portfolio that will perform well over the long term across a range of economic outcomes. As such, our portfolio is diversified across leading franchises earning above-average returns on capital in banking, payments, custody, wealth management and property & casualty insurance. We remain excited by the investment prospects for the companies in Davis Financial Fund.

Together on This Journey

For more than 50 years, Davis Advisors has navigated a constantly changing investment landscape guided by one North Star: to grow the value of the funds entrusted to us. We are pleased to have achieved strong results thus far and look forward to the decades ahead. With more than $2 billion of our own money invested in our portfolios, we stand shoulder to shoulder with our clients on this long journey.3 We are grateful for your trust and are well-positioned for the future.

The average annual total returns for Davis Financial Fund’s Class A shares for periods ending December 31, 2025, including a maximum 4.75% sales charge, are: 1 year, 23.15%; 5 years, 17.16%; and 10 years, 12.25%. The performance presented represents past performance and is not a guarantee of future results. Total return assumes reinvestment of dividends and capital gain distributions.

Investment return and principal value will vary so that, when redeemed, an investor’s shares may be worth more or less than their original cost. data.title: Ovid Therapeutics: Bullish Post-PIPE With 2 Differentiated CNS Moonshots
data.description:
Ovid Therapeutics Inc. (OVID) is a CNS drug developer that focuses on therapies to treat disorders linked to neuronal hyperexcitability. OVID's pipeline is mostly focused on two mechanisms: 1) a GABA-AT inhibitor for uncontrolled epilepsy and 2) KCC2 direct activators. Note that the latter mechanisms target psychosis associated with Parkinson's disease (PD) and Lewy body dementia (LBD). OVID's management anticipates several milestones for its candidates through 2026-2027. And its upside seems compelling, given they estimate a market opportunity for its KCC2 drugs at around $2.5 billion. Thus, I think OVID post-PIPE is now a viable speculative "Buy" for new investors at these levels.

Brain Small Molecules

Ovid Therapeutics Inc. is a biopharmaceutical company that develops small-molecule medicines for brain disorders. They specialize in conditions driven by neuronal hyperexcitability. OVID was founded back in 2014 and is currently headquartered in New York, New York. As a whole, they target sizeable TAMs with unique mechanisms of action, but the stock itself is also quite speculative from a financial perspective. So, it makes for an interesting risk-reward profile that I believe is worth considering.

You see, as a quick overview, OVID's pipeline focuses on two mechanisms of action: a GABA-aminotransferase (GABA-AT) inhibitor and a potassium-chloride cotransporter 2 (KCC2) direct activator. The GABA-AT inhibitor program includes the candidate OV329, which is reporting Phase 1 results for safety, PK, and exposure in healthy volunteers to advance into a Phase 2 trial for drug-resistant adult focal onset seizures (FOS).

It's worth highlighting that OV329's effect increases inhibitory tone and reduces excess neuronal firing. This, in turn, helps reduce seizures, including in patients who present uncontrolled epilepsy, even when using appropriate anti-seizure medicines. This candidate's Phase 2a is scheduled to commence by Q2 2026, and its topline results would come in by mid-2027.

As for OVID's KCC2 program, this one aims to boost KCC2 function to help restore inhibitory control in the brain to modulate neurons that are too excitable. This way, OVID can help regulate and maintain GABA's inhibitory effects. This program includes three candidates. The first candidate, OV350, is an intravenous drug used for the first-in-human activation of KCC2. OVID defines OV350 as a tool to derisk KCC2 in humans, and it has so far completed Phase 1 with a good safety profile and predictable pharmacokinetics. However, OVID doesn't plan to advance OV350 further for now, but its trial results will be used to support the development of other oral candidates.

The second KCC2 program drug is OV4071, which is a medication with oral and injectable options for KCC2 activation. This makes the candidate potentially suitable for psychosis associated with Parkinson's disease, Lewy body dementia (LBD), and schizophrenia. In my view, this particular candidate has a unique and novel mechanism of action that differs from other dopamine-blocking antipsychotics. It's technically an antipsychotic candidate with good tolerability, which allows for doses that render efficacy above the benchmarks, without causing overbearing somnolence.

As for OVID's R&D for PD and LBD, it's important to remember that several standard antipsychotics can worsen motor symptoms, cognition, or overall function, so clinicians are cautious to use them. Therefore, if OV4071 continues to show that it's well tolerated, it could potentially tap into an unmet medical need where generic antipsychotics are problematic. OV4071's IND submission is expected by Q1 2026, and its Phase 1 trial initiation is planned for Q2 2026. And if all goes according to plan, OVID believes they could deliver their topline results by mid-2027.

Aside from those main IPs, OVID has OV4041. This is yet another oral and injectable drug, but it's in preclinical stages for generalized anxiety disorder and Rett syndrome. Its IND submission is anticipated by 2H2026. On paper, OV4041's characteristics include anxiolytic, antipsychotic, and anticonvulsant activity demonstrated in animal models. But since it's still in very early R&D stages, I wouldn't consider it a major value driver to the stock for now.

Valuation and Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, OVID trades at a $220.0 million market cap, placing it decisively in microcap territory. Naturally, this also makes it riskier than larger-cap biotechs, especially since its average trading volume is only 1.6 million shares. Thus, at the current $1.75 PPS, it means OVID is a very thinly traded security with only $2.8 million in trading liquidity. Hence, even small buy/sell orders can significantly push the stock price, which is why you should anticipate OVID remaining volatile for the foreseeable future.

Moreover, the stock is already up around 580% from its 2025 lows, which could signal OVID may be due for a longer period of consolidation. Additionally, their latest 10-Q from Q3 2025 shows their balance sheet holds $20.6 million in cash and $5.0 million in short-term marketable securities. This amounts to approximately $25.6 million in liquid resources against no financial debt besides other regular operating liabilities and total liabilities of $19.2 million.

However, by October 2025, OVID announced a $175 million PIPE to extend its runway into 2028. In theory, the raise is up to $175 million, but what we do know is that initially, they definitely raised at least $81 million in gross proceeds. So, for simplicity, I'll add the initial figure to their previous cash balances and estimate an "updated" cash of around $106.6 million. Similarly, by Q3 2025, OVID's book value reached $44.7 million. But if we add the PIPE funds directly to the equity, it implies an "updated" book value of roughly $125.7 million. Thus, this would imply that OVID trades at a compelling P/B of 1.8. For comparison, its sector's median P/B is closer to 3.0, so I do believe OVID now seems a bit undervalued post-PIPE.

Additionally, I also estimate OVID burned through $12.9 million during Q3 2025. Note that I got this figure by simply adding its quarterly cash flows from operations and CAPEX. And, based on my previously mentioned cash figure, I calculate their "updated" runway at approximately 8.3 quarters, which also aligns with management's cash runway guidance into 2028. In my view, this materially derisked OVID's investment profile, as it shows they should have enough funding to see them through a few key milestones in the near term.

In particular, I would consider OV329 their main value driver, as it targets epilepsy, and we should see its Phase 2a topline data by 1H2027. Their Phase 1 topline presentation showed this candidate has meaningful inhibitory effects compared to placebo, which seems like a very strong signal that this approach might work as intended for patients. Naturally, the Phase 2a data will ultimately give us a much clearer picture of OV329's prospects. If the data shows disappointing performance, it could derail my speculative bull case on this name altogether. But if it comes in with exciting results, I believe that OVID could be much more valuable. In fact, OVID calculated its TAM on epilepsy at around $1.0 billion.

Similarly, OV4071 also has a large market opportunity for PD and LBD, with 1 million patients and 1.4 million patients in the US, respectively. Psychosis symptoms like hallucinations and delusions are common in these diseases (75% of LBD patients and 20% to 40% of PD patients) and require medication. More than 50% of cases with approved treatments don't respond well or cannot tolerate them, which makes it a high unmet medical need demographic. And remember, there are no approved treatments for LBD psychosis.

So, for these two diseases, OVID estimates that its addressable segment is around 840,000 patients, and those who are resistant to available treatments (500,000 with LBD psychosis and 340,000 with PD psychosis). Their investor presentation estimates its revenues could be around $2.5 billion by considering 5% of the addressable population and assuming a treatment price per year of approximately $60,000.

Conclusion: Viable Speculative 'Buy'

Overall, I think OVID post-PIPE is now a much more viable investment candidate at these levels. From a valuation angle, the stock itself seems to trade at cheap multiples. However, when you consider the impressive optionality of its candidates, I think OVID becomes an exceedingly compelling speculative "Buy" at these levels. Yet, since its success ultimately rides on successful clinical trials and data, it's inherently very uncertain and risky. After all, their success is not guaranteed, and over time, OVID's cash burn will pile up, and without further promising data, the stock could ultimately lead to substantial shareholder losses. That's why I can only rate it a speculative "Buy" for now until we get more concrete results on its candidates. So, make sure you keep your position size manageable until we see more data on their promising pipeline candidates.

data.title: Orion Property: Strategic Alternatives Takes Center Stage
data.description:
Orion Properties (ONL) announced a strategic alternatives process. This comes after the last article covered a "going concern" statement. Most likely the issues around the going concern statement are causing at least some of what is going on. There is a possibility that management is not satisfied with the financing options available.

What needs to be considered is that this company originally came into being as the "leftovers" of a company that was sold to Realty Income (O) and what Realty Income likewise contributed to the company. This management does have a sale to Realty Income as a historical accomplishment. That by no means guarantees success. But it does mean that this management likely possesses the management ability to avoid a bankruptcy filing for any reason.

Now, the "strategic alternatives" combined with the "going concern" in the financials does imply a certain risk level that many investors will not be happy with. What needs to happen here is that the revolver which has most of the company debt needs a far more certain future than it has now or the stock price is not likely to wander too far from current levels and could actually decline from these levels if events "head South" from here.

My own feeling is that this company needs an acquisition or two to dig its way out of the current situation. A company that is made up of "leftovers" as is the case here from another company sale just may have a bit too much baggage that needs more time to work itself out of the situation.

Economic Considerations

What may be complicating the issue is the macro environment that is beyond the control of management.

Lately, the government policies seem to be contrary to everything that I have learned in my economics classes (and economics is one of my three areas in my MBA).

Generally, the way it works is that someone learns the "rules" in any situation so well that those rules are applied in a way that no one saw coming to bring about a success that no one thought was possible.

But the current situation seems to be different in that the "rules" are being ignored as if all the research done is useless and anyone can be an expert whose opinions are equal to those that spent years in economics. But such a situation has a lot going against it in terms of success.

Tariffs for example in my old Samuelson textbook titled "Economics" are basically defined as a tax upon us for certain imports. But a tax increase is also noted as a "sure thing" that weighs in to slow economic growth and possibly bring about a recession.

Now economics has no time element in its research because results depend very much upon what others do. Therefore, one thing that weighs in to bring about a recession (and hence empty office buildings that are anathema to this company) may in fact not predominate. All we have right now is one factor that does not look good. It does not help that the tariff situation keeps changing. That just adds to the future uncertainty.

Similarly, increasing deficits and low interest rates are almost "guaranteed" to build inflationary pressures. Tariffs also weigh in to magnify this effect as well. But pressure is not the same as it actually happening.

Now inflation is generally good for real estate values and could in the long run help the financial situation with the "going concern" issues as well as the strategic evaluation. But usually, the challenges come first before the help.

The whole political bargaining that is marked by talk of tariffs and trade wars is yet another consideration that threatens to cause a recession or worse (along with more office building vacancies).

Therefore, behind the strategic alternatives is a desire for some long-term financing that would get us well beyond the current political uncertainty (completely with a political thunderstorm possibility ahead). Management may well have determined that the current challenging situation may last much longer than originally mattered because of the political situation.

The key to all of this is that many who voted for president in the last election did so on the hope that he would solve all of their problems, including the challenges noted for this company. But the reverse seems to be happening. That may have caused a course correction (strategically speaking) from a turnaround strategy to a "get out" strategy. Time will tell on this.

As of right now, management appears to be voting "no" through its actions on the current political situation. Time will tell how all of this works out and if that "no" stands up.

Finances And Strategy

As of the end of the third quarter, here is a summary of the company situation:

Followed by here is what has gotten done in summary fashion since the company came into being:

The key issue here is the "dedicated use" as that is likely the way forward and the percentage that needs to increase substantially. But that also implies a whole lot of changes going forward.

It is very likely that the company has properties that were once "good" and are now next to useless or generic. But that is not a good situation to be in when vacancies are an issue.

The way out, as was discussed early in the article is to get those in demand "dedicated use" properties to be a larger part of the portfolio while quickly getting rid of as many "generic" or noncore properties as possible. This is what may have gotten interrupted by the current political situation.

The last set of challenges revolves around the debt due shown in the slide above.

It is of course possible that the company will find an acquisition that also solves the debt issues at the same time. That has happened more than once. But much depends upon management and the opportunities available.

Summary

This management has noted that it had already turned down some bids for the company because those bids substantially undervalued the company. Now it is possible that the company is doing the "strategic alternatives" in the hope of maximizing the company value now rather than later.

My own suspicion is that a "now" substantially undervalues what the company can get later. But the finances are such that a "now" may be the only practical way of doing things. Sometimes there is an element present that wants the profits "now" rather than taking a chance on greater profits later.

So, there is a lot to consider here as this is now a very speculative situation. This has probably not gone the way management had envisioned when it originally agreed to take control of this situation a few years back. Much of it is beyond the control of management.

However, from the current price, whatever happens should be a good outcome for investors. However, the risk is high as we are in uncharted territory for what continues to be a strong buy idea but now that idea has a very high level of risk. It may have therefore become unsuitable for a lot of investors.

On the other hand, we should know the outcome of the strategic alternatives fairly quickly.

Risks

The biggest risk is the political situation that may cause a recession as discussed previously. This needs to be replaced with something that is permanent, certain, and constructive.

The going concern statement probably has a better than average chance to be successfully resolved in the favor of management. However, that statement alone (not including the political situation) could make this stock unacceptable to many due to the high risk regardless of the outcome.

The "strategic alternatives" announcement may or may not result in a clear pathway forward. This company may be in for more challenges than originally anticipated.

data.title: 'Sell The Dollar': Think Price, Not Flows
data.description:
The “sell the dollar” trade is coming up in the chatter I see. I think that economists love making international trade and finance more complicated than it should be, but that is partly a side effect of needing to publish new articles on a continuous basis. My simplistic input on this topic is that people need to stop thinking about “global rebalancing” in terms of flows; price changes can do the job.

My guess is that the focus on flows draws from the experience of developing markets, with countries with less developed financial sectors. If a handful of rich people directly control most of the assets in a country, it is a lot easier to generate catastrophic herding behaviour. When financial assets are in the hands of professional investors, there is a more diffuse response, as many professional investors are liability matchers that have the capacity to go against the herd.

Price Changes Do the Job

Let us imagine that we are a foreign investor with a portfolio that has a weighting in U.S. dollar (USD) assets of 30%. (Although foreign investors might have a higher weighting in U.S. equities, fixed income assets have a home bias due to liability matching.) Let us further imagine that said foreign investors are spooked by the arbitrary legal manoeuvres of the White House and want to reduce their USD weighting to 25%.

The hard way to do that is to do a portfolio analysis and move 5% of their portfolio out of the U.S.

The easy way to do it is to wake up one morning and find that the local currency value of their USD portfolio has dropped by 22%. The value change can be accomplished by the dollar tanking or the assets falling in USD terms. Given the importance of equities in portfolios, generating valuation changes of that magnitude is not that hard. Besides the possible situation of Japanese investors, most foreign real money managers have relatively short-duration USD fixed income assets. (The exception would be a portfolio that has no USD assets in their benchmark, and their USD exposure would only be relative value overlay trades, which could be long duration. This would not be surprising for Canadian fixed income managers. However, risk budgets are finite, and the allocation to such overlay trades is going to have a small notional amount when compared to the portfolio size.)

Markets are like movie theatres. Fun to be in, at least until someone yells “Fire!” At which point, the exits are far too small for the number of patrons in the theatre.

USD Would Not Be “Replaced,” Just Shrunk (In The Short Run…)

There has long been a strand of analysis that went along the lines that the USD will not be replaced as the “global reserve currency” because other markets are “too small.” This analysis is arguably correct, but the point of failure is that “global reserve currency” is a very squishy qualitative concept. The weighting of USD in international transactions can be accomplished by shrinking the volume of international transactions and other currencies just increasing their weighting at the expense of USD.

Unless one can find a country that has been running surpluses for some time (Australia was in that boat some time back), local fixed income markets are going to be “big enough” for local investors. Sure, American investors might find investing in CAD bonds unattractive, but all that is needed that CAD investors do (and they are already major investors).

Japanese Investors?

The only fixed income investors I see running for the door are Japanese investors who had large weightings in U.S. fixed income because Japanese yields were structurally lower than U.S. yields. The yield gap has been fixed, so there is an incentive to bring weightings home. At the same time, they realise that it is not in their interest to set their portfolios’ value on fire, so my default assumption is that any withdrawal would be relatively orderly.

Will There Be Massive Flows Out of the U.S.?

Accounting tells us that about the only way to engineer large foreign outflows from the U.S. is for the U.S. to switch to a trade surplus. One of the structural problems of popular macro analysis is the inability to match buyers and sellers. People (including some academic economists…) seem to think about finance like their own personal portfolio, where they just hit “buy” and “sell” buttons.

An American trade deficit is the situation where Americans are net selling U.S. dollars in exchange for goods and services. The only other part of international accounts big enough to absorb that dollar selling is the capital account — buying and selling the currency to buy/sell U.S. and foreign currency financial assets. So, as long as there is a trade deficit in goods and services, there has to be a net inflow into USD assets.

We could see U.S. investors repatriating foreign currency assets to cover the gap. I do not pay too much attention to international financial statistics, but U.S. investors were historically notable for their home bias. It is unclear to me how large repatriation flows could be.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
